text: Except your way forward. Thank you. Thank you, Brazil. Dominic Abyss. Thank you, Mr. Chair. And thank you all delegates for this interesting discussion. We've been patiently sitting here the week to really digest what position to take and we still need to get our heads around this a little bit. As an island nation and how this concept really affects this small struggling island nation and its economy. When I have patiently listened to the interventions over this this past week I've given pause in you know thank you Nigeria for such an articulate intervention few minutes ago that I couldn't appreciate an intervention more than what I listened to from Nigeria that was that was substantial for a small island nation like ours. I'm worried about this and worried about it in how it affects our economy in Dominic. I'm worried about how it affects the economies in the south Pacific. I see a room divided for sure and I see I see some things that I hadn't seen at IMO and you know the Secretary General talked about coming here for 27 years and I got you beat a couple of years. Been coming here since 1996 first on the US delegation and then Dominica. This is a different vibe in this room and it does not feel like the IMO that I have attended to since I was a young man and what I really hope is that everybody considers every single member states voice and the importance of that their vote. The United States last intervention I'll be it very fast I guess trying to fit it in with this three-minute rule which bothers me quite frankly. That was an articulate intervention in the words within that intervention were cogent for all of us to listen to. So I beg of everybody to really think this through in a way that is constructive for all member states to realize the importance of a carbon neutral world but also the importance of our decisions in how it affects our economies and the stability of our economies and the stability of global trade in the industry. I'm worried thank you for allowing me to share. Thank you Dominica. Nadakiman please. Thank you chair. The UK aligns with Denmark, Netherlands and Brazil and supports forwarding the papers you have referenced for further discussion to the working group where we hope to have a rich and useful discussion. It is indeed the case that the work plan is not legally binding only the net zero framework is legally binding and we have yet to take a final decision on that as I think we are all aware. But many have said how important it is that we understand the road ahead. It is therefore right that we thoughtfully discuss the work plan this week as envisaged within the agenda. It is standard practice within this committee to plot out milestones in advance and this is what we have done previously for example in relation to the 2023 strategy. We would like to thank member states who have submitted papers commenting on the work plan for their constructive and considered engagement. We would also like to thank all those who have raised further considerations in their statements yesterday. It is important that we consider how these can be fully worked through and we can't do that if we don't have a discussion this week. Thank you Nadakiman. Czaman, please. Thank you chair. We know that we have a lot of work ahead of us and this is why we would like to thank explicitly the secretaryate for this very helpful draft work plan as said out in MEPC ES23 which we support in general. In case of the adoption of the framework we need a work plan, a planning tool, a map for the way forward which we still want to go together. We are hearing the concerns and we take them very serious but at the same time we have heard a lot of commitment the last two days to multilateralism, to talking and listening to each other. The secretaryate general explicitly asked us to do so. It is just that we will be able to make this meeting a second. And so we support to allow the work in group to do the necessary preparatory work. This is why we would like to align ourselves with the statements made by Denmark, the Netherlands, Brazil and the UK. Thank you very much. Thank you, Chairman. Thank you, Chairman. Thank you, Chairman. Thank you chair. Finald would like to thank the secretaryate for the draft work plan provided in document MEPC ES23 and supported in general. After hearing your decision chair that all documents related to the work plan will be sent to the working group, I can be brief. And align ourselves with the statements by Denmark, the Netherlands and Brazil. Thank you chair. Thank you, Phil. Italy, please. Thank you chair. Italy, align itself with the statement made by Denmark, UK, Netherlands, Brazil and the others and support the work plan. Thank you. Tanzania, please. Thank you chair. The delegation of you, the United Republic of Tanzania, comments the secretaryate for its comprehensive draft work plan to guide the organization's preparations for the entry into force of the IAM-1A0 framework. Tanzania recognizes that the proposed work plan presents an important step towards structured and coordinated implementation of the revised MAPO-RNX625 and appreciates the secretary's efforts to outline timelines and interlinkages across the various greenhouse gas work streams. While noting the plan's indicative nature, Tanzania emphasizes the importance of ensuring that all preparatory work under the IAM-1A0 framework is guided by the principle of equity, common but differentiated responsibilities and respective capabilities, particularly for African states, least developed capital countries and small island developing states. We stress the establishment of the IAM-1A0GFI-reducery and the IAM-1A0FAN must incorporate mechanisms for capacity building, financial accessibility, and fair costs recovery to avoid the creating disproportionate patterns on developing maritime constructions and smaller ship registries. Tanzania supports continued deliberations on the development of the lifecycle assessment framework, sustainable fuel certification schemes, and fuel lifecycle labels while underlining the need, original collaboration and recognition of fuel certification infrastructure in Africa. The delegation also welcomes the inclusion of dedicated work stream on food security impacts, noting the particular vulnerability of input-dependent economies to changes in maritime transport costs and encourages the secretary to ensure that such assessments remain transparent and inclusive. Tanzania underscores that the implementation of the IAM-1A0 framework should proceed in a first balanced and transparent manner, allowing sufficient time for developing states to strengthen institutional capacity, legislation and port-yredness. In this regard Tanzania reiterates the need for technical cooperation and financial support through the IAM-1A0GFI-reducery and the IAM-1A0GFI-GFI-reducery trust fund in line with the principle of a just and equitable transition. The delegation remains committed to working constructively with member states to ensure that the global maritime decabonization process is both effective and fair, contributing to sustainable development in all regions. Thank you, Chair. Thank you, Tanzania. Looking at the time, I think it's time for coffee. I'm going to a break now and then I will come back and continue taking the cars and then I think the floor. Chair, please. La delegation de Tiro is the chair, which is to acknowledge the work of all the distinguished allegations and of course the secretary and especially thank your stewardship of the meeting over these last few days, Chair. In order to recharge batteries and continue working in a fine spirit we'd like to invite you to come to as well if you prefer it, Chair. So you're all
